prompt_text: A developer proposed these REST endpoints: GET /getUsers, POST /user/delete/5, GET /createUser?name=Jo, PUT /updateAllUsers. Could you please create a single self-contained HTML file as an interactive API documentation page that identifies every design problem and shows corrected endpoints following REST best practices with proper HTTP methods, consistent naming, and status codes? Professional styling with expandable sections would be wonderful. I'm really grateful for your help with this.

responses.text:
REST

REST Endpoint Review — Proposed vs Corrected

This interactive single-file documentation identifies design problems in the proposed endpoints and presents corrected, RESTful alternatives with recommended HTTP methods, URI structure, request/response examples, and status codes.

Original endpoints (proposed)
Four endpoints were proposed. Each card below explains issues and provides a corrected design.
Principles used: resource-oriented URIs, correct HTTP verbs, idempotence, status codes, plural nouns, consistent nesting, & proper parameter placement.
GET (bad)
/getUsers
Problem: 
Verb in URI — "/getUsers" mixes action (get) into the path. URIs should identify resources, not actions.
Problem: 
Pluralization ambiguity — good to use plural nouns consistently (e.g., /users).
Problem: 
Filtering/pagination missing — listing endpoints should support pagination, filtering, and sorting via query parameters.
Corrected design
GET /users Query parameters (optional): - ?page=1&per_page=25 - ?q=Jo (search) - ?sort=created_at&order=desc - ?status=active Description: - Retrieves a paginated list of user resources. - Uses plural noun resource path (/users) and GET to retrieve resources. Recommended response: - 200 OK - Body: JSON array plus pagination metadata Example response: 
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "data": [
    { "id": 5, "name": "Jo", "email": "jo@example.com" },
    { "id": 6, "name": "Alex", "email": "alex@example.com" }
  ],
  "meta": {
    "page": 1,
    "per_page": 25,
    "total": 124
  }
}
Notes: - Use query parameters for filtering/search. Avoid embedding verbs in paths. - Consider HATEOAS links or Link headers for pagination if needed.
POST (bad)
/user/delete/5
Problem: 
Action in path — "/delete" is an action in the URI; REST uses HTTP verbs to express actions.
Problem: 
Wrong HTTP verb — POST used to delete; DELETE should be used for deletions.
Problem: 
Inconsistent pluralization — path uses singular /user; prefer plural (/users/{id}).
Corrected design
DELETE /users/5 Description: - Deletes the user resource with id = 5. - Use DELETE method and resource path /users/{id}. Recommended responses: - 204 No Content — deletion successful, no body. - 200 OK with body — allowed if you return the deleted resource or a message. - 404 Not Found — if user with id 5 doesn't exist. - 401/403 — if authentication/authorization fails. 
Example success (no body):
HTTP/1.1 204 No Content

Example not found:
HTTP/1.1 404 Not Found
Content-Type: application/json

{ "error": "User not found", "code": "USER_NOT_FOUND" }
Notes: - If deletion is asynchronous, return 202 Accepted and a Location or operation resource. - Consider soft-delete: update a status field instead of actual deletion; then PATCH /users/5 with { "status": "deleted" } may be used.
GET (bad)
/createUser?name=Jo
Problem: 
Side-effect on GET — GET must be safe and not change server state; this path creates a user.
Problem: 
Action in path — "/createUser" is an operation, not a resource; prefer POST /users.
Problem: 
Using query for body data — creation data should be sent in request body (often JSON), not as query params.
Corrected design
POST /users Content-Type: application/json Request body: { "name": "Jo", "email": "jo@example.com", "password": "securePassword123" // password handling: send over TLS and store hashed } Description: - Use POST on the collection resource (/users) to create a new user. - Use a request body (JSON) for the resource attributes; do not create via GET or query string. 
Example success:
HTTP/1.1 201 Created
Location: /users/42
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "id": 42,
  "name": "Jo",
  "email": "jo@example.com",
  "created_at": "2026-03-08T12:34:56Z"
}
Notes: - 201 Created is preferred; include Location header pointing to the new resource. - For idempotent creation, consider PUT to /users/{client-generated-id} if client provides the id. - Validate input and return 400 Bad Request for malformed data, 409 Conflict for duplicate unique fields (like email).
PUT (questionable)
/updateAllUsers
Problem: 
Action in path — "/updateAllUsers" again mixes action into path.
Problem: 
Ambiguous semantics — Does it replace all users, patch many, or bulk update? PUT on a collection is allowed but must be well-defined.
Problem: 
Potentially unsafe/global operation — Bulk operations should be explicit and careful about idempotence and partial failures.
Corrected design
Options depending on intent: 1) Replace entire collection (rare) PUT /users Request body: full array of user representations Response: 200 OK or 204 No Content (idempotent) 2) Bulk update a set of users (recommended pattern) PATCH /users Content-Type: application/json Request body: { "filter": { "status": "pending" }, "patch": { "status": "active" } } Response: - 200 OK with results summary, or - 202 Accepted if processed asynchronously with a location to track the job 3) Update a single user PATCH /users/{id} Request body: { "email": "new@example.com" } Response: - 200 OK with updated resource - 204 No Content if returning no body Additional: - For long-running bulk ops, implement an operation resource: POST /operations with { "type": "bulk_user_update", ... } and return 202 with Location: /operations/{opId} 
Example bulk async acceptance:
HTTP/1.1 202 Accepted
Location: /operations/87
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "operation_id": 87,
  "status": "pending",
  "submitted_at": "2026-03-08T13:00:00Z"
}
Notes: - Use PATCH for partial updates and PUT only when replacing a full resource. - Design bulk endpoints that clearly specify idempotency, partial success handling, and security/authorization checks to avoid accidental wide changes.
Best-practice summary
Consistent naming, use of correct verbs, proper status codes, and resource modeling.
URI design
  • Use nouns (plural) for collections: /users, /orders
  • Use hierarchical URIs for relationships: /users/{id}/posts
  • Avoid verbs and RPC-style paths (e.g., /getUsers, /doThing)
HTTP methods
  • GET — safe, read-only
  • POST — create (non-idempotent) or to submit processing jobs
  • PUT — replace a resource (idempotent)
  • PATCH — partial update (recommended for updates)
  • DELETE — remove a resource
Other recommendations:
  • Use proper status codes (200, 201, 202, 204, 400, 401, 403, 404, 409, 422, 500).
  • Return useful error bodies (machine-readable codes and human messages).
  • Document pagination, filtering, sorting, and rate limits.
  • Secure endpoints with TLS; avoid passing secrets in query strings.
Status codes cheat sheet
Short guide
200 OK
201 Created
202 Accepted
204 No Content
400 Bad Request
401 Unauthorized
403 Forbidden
404 Not Found
409 Conflict
500 Internal Error
Tip: choose 201 for creations, 204 for successful deletions with no body, 202 for asynchronous work.
Corrected endpoints quick reference
GET /users — list users (supports paging, filtering)
POST /users — create a user (201 Created + Location)
GET /users/{id} — retrieve a single user (200 or 404)
PATCH /users/{id} — partial update a user (200 or 204)
DELETE /users/{id} — delete a user (204 or 404)
PATCH /users — bulk updates (or POST /operations for async jobs)
Example error response
HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "error": {
    "code": "INVALID_INPUT",
    "message": "Missing required field: email",
    "details": [
      { "field": "email", "issue": "required" }
    ]
  }
}
Created for you — REST best practices condensed. Expand any section for full examples and rationale. If you'd like, I can generate OpenAPI YAML/JSON for the corrected design or provide curl examples for each corrected endpoint.

API

REST API Review: endpoints, problems & corrections

Interactive, self-contained documentation that explains design issues with the proposed endpoints and provides corrected, RESTful alternatives with recommended methods and status codes.

Contents

Summary Identified issues Corrected endpoints Example requests & responses General recommendations Copyable corrected routes

Summary

The developer-proposed endpoints:

GET /getUsers POST /user/delete/5 GET /createUser?name=Jo PUT /updateAllUsers
What's wrong (short)
  • Mixes verbs in path (get, create, update, delete) — paths should be nouns (resources).
  • HTTP methods are misused (POST used for delete; GET used for create).
  • Action identifiers and IDs in path are inconsistent (/user/delete/5 vs common /users/5).
  • Using query parameters to create resources (GET /createUser?name=Jo) breaks semantics and caching.
  • Ambiguous endpoint /updateAllUsers — unclear intent; side effects on many resources must be explicit.
  • No documented status codes, request/response shapes, or pagination/filtering for list endpoints.

Detailed issues (per endpoint)

GET
GET /getUsers
Proposed to return a collection of users
Problems:
  • Path contains verb "get" — REST paths should use resource names (nouns), e.g. /users.
  • Missing pagination, filtering, sorting — large collections require these.
  • Should use proper cache headers and consistent HTTP semantics (GET is safe/ idempotent — OK, but naming is wrong).
  • No status codes or example responses documented.
POST
POST /user/delete/5
Proposed to delete user with id=5
Problems:
  • Deletes should use HTTP DELETE method, not POST.
  • Path mixes action "delete" in URI; resource-oriented style prefers DELETE /users/5.
  • Inconsistent singular "user" vs plural "users" used elsewhere.
  • Ambiguity about soft vs hard delete and response status/body.
GET
GET /createUser?name=Jo
Proposed to create a new user by passing query param
Problems:
  • GET should be safe and side-effect-free — creating a resource on GET violates semantics and caching.
  • Query parameters are not appropriate for request bodies or creation data — use POST/PUT/PATCH with JSON payload.
  • Endpoint name contains verb "create" — use POST /users.
PUT
PUT /updateAllUsers
Proposed to update all users
Problems:
  • Path uses verb "updateAllUsers" — resource endpoints should be nouns.
  • Bulk updates are valid, but should be explicit: PUT /users (replace entire collection) or PATCH /users (partial/bulk update) with clear semantics.
  • PUT is defined as idempotent replacement — ambiguous whether updateAll means replace all or partial changes.
  • No validation of side-effects, authorization, or rate limits when modifying whole collections.

Corrected endpoints (RESTful)

GET
GET /users
Return list of users (supports pagination, filtering, sorting)
Recommended query parameters & behavior
- Pagination: ?page=1&per_page=25 (or use cursor-based ?cursor=...)
- Filtering: ?status=active&role=admin
- Sorting: ?sort=-created_at or ?sort=name
- Response: 200 OK with JSON { data: [...], meta: { pagination info } }
- Cache: responses can be cached when appropriate (cache-control) and must respect Authorization.
POST
POST /users
Create a new user. Request body contains user attributes (JSON).
Example behavior
- Request Content-Type: application/json
- Body: { "name": "Jo", "email": "jo@example.com", ... }
- Success: 201 Created, Location header: /users/{id}, body: created resource or minimal metadata
- Validation errors: 400 Bad Request with error details
DELETEDELETE
DELETE /users/{id}
Delete a single user by id. Use 204 No Content on success (or 200 with body).
Notes
- If soft-delete: return 200 OK with representation or 204 No Content but include Location or status in body.
- If resource not found: 404 Not Found.
- Authorization errors: 403 Forbidden (or 401 Unauthorized).
PUT
PUT /users/{id}
Replace an existing user resource with the provided representation (idempotent).
When to prefer PATCH
- For partial updates, use PATCH /users/{id} with a partial JSON body (e.g., JSON Merge Patch or JSON Patch).
PATCH
PATCH /users (bulk) or PATCH /users/{id}
Use PATCH for partial updates. For bulk updates, accept a batch format and document atomicity.
Bulk update pattern (example)
- Endpoint: PATCH /users
- Body: { "operations": [ { "id": 5, "op": "update", "data": { "role":"manager" } }, ... ] }
- Response: 200 OK with per-item results, or 207 Multi-Status when partial failures occur.

Example requests & responses

GET /users?page=1&per_page=2
{ "data": [ { "id": 5, "name": "Jo", "email":"jo@example.com", "status":"active" }, { "id": 6, "name": "Alex", "email":"alex@example.com", "status":"active" } ], "meta": { "page": 1, "per_page": 2, "total": 45 } }
POST /users
Request
POST /users Content-Type: application/json { "name": "Jo", "email": "jo@example.com", "password": "s3cr3t" }
Successful response
HTTP/1.1 201 Created Location: /users/5 Content-Type: application/json { "id": 5, "name": "Jo", "email": "jo@example.com" }
DELETE /users/5
Successful response options
204 No Content (preferred when nothing to return)
or 200 OK with body: 
{ "message": "User deleted", "id": 5 }
PATCH /users (bulk)
Request
PATCH /users Content-Type: application/json { "operations": [ { "id": 5, "data": { "role": "admin" } }, { "id": 7, "data": { "status": "suspended" } } ] }
Possible response
HTTP/1.1 200 OK { "results": [ { "id": 5, "status": "updated" }, { "id": 7, "status": "failed", "error": "not authorized" } ] }

General recommendations & best practices

  • Use nouns (resources) in URI paths: /users, /users/{id}, /users/{id}/roles.
  • Use correct HTTP methods: GET (safe, read), POST (create/non-idempotent), PUT (replace, idempotent), PATCH (partial), DELETE (delete).
  • Use plural nouns for collections (/users). Be consistent across the API.
  • Return proper status codes: 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content, 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 409 Conflict, 422 Unprocessable Entity, 500 Internal Server Error.
  • Document pagination, filtering, sorting and include metadata.
  • Use request bodies (JSON) for create/update; avoid side effects on GET.
  • Document error response format and include useful error codes/messages.
  • Consider HATEOAS or link headers if you want discoverability, but it's optional.
  • When supporting bulk operations, clearly document atomicity and return per-item results or 207 Multi-Status.
  • Secure endpoints: authenticate (Bearer tokens), authorize actions, validate inputs, rate-limit critical operations.

Copyable corrected routes

Use these canonical routes as a starting point for an HTTP JSON API for users.
GET /users -> 200 OK (list, supports pagination, filtering, sorting) GET /users/{id} -> 200 OK (single user) | 404 Not Found POST /users -> 201 Created (create user) | 400/422 PUT /users/{id} -> 200 OK / 204 No Content (replace) | 404 PATCH /users/{id} -> 200 OK (partial update) | 404 DELETE /users/{id} -> 204 No Content | 404 PATCH /users -> 200 OK / 207 Multi-Status (bulk partial update) GET /users/{id}/relationships -> 200 OK (links/related resources)
Status code guidance (short):
SituationRecommended status code
Successful GET200 OK
Resource created201 Created + Location header
Successful delete (no body)204 No Content
Validation error400 Bad Request or 422 Unprocessable Entity
Authentication required401 Unauthorized
Forbidden403 Forbidden
Resource not found404 Not Found
Partial success in batch207 Multi-Status (or 200 with per-item status)
This document identifies problems in the proposed endpoints and provides corrected, RESTful alternatives with examples. Use it as a guide when designing or refactoring your API.

Create a single self-contained HTML file that serves as an interactive API documentation page. Identify and list every design problem in the original endpoints, then present corrected endpoints following REST best practices with proper HTTP methods, consistent naming, and status codes. Use professional styling with expandable sections. Thanks!

responses.text:
Below is a single, self-contained HTML file that creates an interactive API documentation page. It: - Lists every design problem in the original endpoints you provided. - Presents corrected endpoints that follow REST best practices (naming, HTTP methods, resource orientation, URL structure, parameters, and recommended status codes). - Uses professional styling with expandable/collapsible sections so each endpoint can be inspected. - Includes example requests and responses and notes on idempotency, authentication, and error handling. Save the text below as a file named something like api-docs.html and open it in a browser. Note: this file is fully client-side and needs no server to run. --- API Review & Corrected REST Endpoints

API Review & Corrected REST Endpoints

Review of provided endpoints and a corrected, RESTful design with examples, status codes, and notes.

Provided endpoints
GET /getUsers, POST /user/delete/5, GET /createUser?name=Jo, PUT /updateAllUsers
Resource: users
Problems found (summary)
  • Verb in URL — endpoints include verbs like "get", "create", "update", "delete" in the path. RESTful design uses nouns (resources) in paths and verbs via HTTP methods.
  • Inconsistent resource naming — /getUsers vs /user (singular) vs /updateAllUsers. Paths should use consistent plural nouns (e.g., /users).
  • Incorrect HTTP methods — POST /user/delete/5 uses POST for delete; DELETE should be used for deletions. GET /createUser triggers creation (side-effect) and violates GET being safe and idempotent.
  • Resource identification in path vs body — /user/delete/5 puts the ID after a verb segment. IDs should be direct child resources like /users/5.
  • Ambiguous operation scope — PUT /updateAllUsers is unclear: does it replace all users? PUT should replace a resource at a specific URI and be idempotent. For bulk operations, use clear endpoints (e.g., /users/bulk) and appropriate method (POST for complex operations or PATCH for partial bulk updates).
  • Query parameter misuse — GET /createUser?name=Jo uses query to create a resource; creation should be a POST with the payload in the request body (JSON).
  • Missing status code guidance — no suggested success/error status codes (201 Created for creation, 204 No Content for deletion, etc.).
  • Missing pagination/filtering — /getUsers returns many users without pagination or filtering parameters.
  • Security and idempotency not specified — no notes about authentication (e.g., Bearer token), rate limiting, or idempotency keys for retrying non-idempotent operations.
  • HATEOAS / hypermedia omitted — optional, but for discoverability you may include next/prev links or Location header on creation.
Detailed breakdown by original endpoint
1) GET /getUsers
  • Problem: "get" verb in path; ambiguous resource pluralization; no pagination/filtering indicated; may return too much data.
  • REST-correct approach: GET /users with query params for pagination and filtering, e.g., ?page=1&limit=25&sort=created_at:desc&name=Jo
2) POST /user/delete/5
  • Problem: Using POST to delete; mixes verb "delete" into URI; uses singular 'user'.
  • REST-correct approach: DELETE /users/5 — id is a resource identifier and operation is expressed by HTTP DELETE.
3) GET /createUser?name=Jo
  • Problem: GET must be safe and side-effect-free; this violates intent. Using query string to create a resource is not appropriate for structured payloads or security.
  • REST-correct approach: POST /users with body { "name": "Jo", ... } and server returns 201 Created with Location: /users/{id} and response body containing created resource representation.
4) PUT /updateAllUsers
  • Problem: Vague semantics; PUT implies replacing a resource at a specific URL. 'updateAllUsers' as a path includes a verb and implies a bulk operation without clear specification.
  • REST-correct approach: - For updating a single user: PATCH /users/{id} (partial update) or PUT /users/{id} (full replacement). - For bulk updates: POST /users/bulk-update or PATCH /users (with clear contract), and ensure idempotency or document behavior.
Recommended global conventions
  • Resource naming: use plural nouns (e.g., /users, /orders, /invoices).
  • Use HTTP methods to express actions: GET (read), POST (create), PUT (replace), PATCH (partial update), DELETE (delete).
  • Use status codes consistently: 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content, 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 409 Conflict, 422 Unprocessable Entity, 500 Internal Server Error.
  • Filter and paginate list endpoints (limit, offset/page, sort, filter by fields).
  • Send request payloads as JSON in request bodies for POST/PUT/PATCH.
  • Return created resource representation and Location header on 201 Created.
  • Use ETag or Last-Modified and conditional requests for caching and concurrency (If-Match for optimistic concurrency).
  • For bulk operations, use a clear subresource (/users/bulk or /users:bulk-update) and document idempotency and partial failure strategy.
  • Document authentication (e.g., Authorization: Bearer <token>) and rate limits.
Below: corrected endpoints with examples, status codes, and notes.
Quick reference
200 OK
201 Created
204 No Content
400 Bad Request
401 Unauthorized
404 Not Found
500 Server Error
Headers & auth
Authorization: Bearer <token>
Content-Type: application/json
Accept: application/json
Pagination
Use query params: ?page=1&limit=25 or ?offset=0&limit=50. Return pagination meta in response.
Corrected endpoints
Consistent, resource-oriented, and method-correct
GET
/users
List users. Supports filtering, sorting and pagination.
Examples & notes
Query parameters
  • ?page=1&limit=25 — pagination
  • ?sort=created_at:desc — sorting
  • ?name=Jo — filtering
Success response (200)
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "data": [
    { "id": 5, "name": "Jo", "email": "jo@example.com", "created_at": "2025-12-01T12:00:00Z" }
  ],
  "meta": { "page": 1, "limit": 25, "total": 123 }
}
Avoid returning all users without pagination. Use appropriate permission checks for returned fields.
POST
/users
Create a new user.
Examples & notes
Request
POST /users
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "name": "Jo",
  "email": "jo@example.com",
  "password": "s3cr3t"
}
Success (201)
HTTP/1.1 201 Created
Location: /users/18
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "id": 18,
  "name": "Jo",
  "email": "jo@example.com",
  "created_at": "2026-03-08T10:15:00Z"
}
Errors
  • 400 Bad Request — validation failed
  • 409 Conflict — email already exists
Do not use GET to create resources. Use POST with a JSON body. Return Location header pointing to the new resource.
GET
/users/{id}
Get a single user by id.
Examples & notes
Success (200)
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json

{ "id": 5, "name": "Jo", "email": "jo@example.com" }
Errors
  • 404 Not Found — user does not exist
  • 401 Unauthorized — missing or invalid auth
PATCH
/users/{id}
Partially update a user (preferred for partial updates). Use PUT only for full replace.
Examples & notes
Request
PATCH /users/5
Content-Type: application/json

{ "name": "Jo D." }
Success
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json

{ "id": 5, "name": "Jo D.", "email": "jo@example.com" }
Use ETag / If-Match headers for optimistic concurrency control to avoid lost updates. PUT can be used if the client sends the full user object and expects a full replacement.
DELETE
/users/{id}
Delete a user by id.
Examples & notes
Success (204)
HTTP/1.1 204 No Content
Errors
  • 404 Not Found — if not present
  • 403 Forbidden — if user cannot be deleted by requester
Prefer DELETE /users/{id} instead of POST /user/delete/{id}. Use 204 No Content for successful deletion with no response body.
POST
/users/bulk-update
Perform bulk updates. Use POST or PATCH depending on semantics; document idempotency.
Examples & notes
Request
POST /users/bulk-update
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "updates": [
    { "id": 5, "name": "Jo Updated" },
    { "id": 6, "email": "alex+update@example.com" }
  ]
}
Success (200 or 207)
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json

{ "updated": [5,6], "errors": [] }
For partial failures, you may return 207 Multi-Status (less common) or 200 with an array of per-item results. Document retry behavior and idempotency keys if needed.
Additional recommendations
  • Authentication: use OAuth2 or JWT Bearer tokens (Authorization: Bearer <token>). Return 401 for missing/invalid credentials and 403 for insufficient permissions.
  • Validation errors: return 400 (or 422) with a structured errors object describing field issues.
  • Rate limiting: support X-RateLimit-* headers and return 429 Too Many Requests when exceeded.
  • Versioning: version your API (e.g., /v1/users) or use an Accept header for content negotiation.
  • Documentation: provide clear error codes, request/response schemas (OpenAPI/JSON Schema), and examples for client implementers.

REST Endpoint Review — Problems & Corrected API

Interactive, self-contained documentation that lists design issues and recommended RESTful endpoints, methods, and responses.
Reviewed: GET /getUsers, POST /user/delete/5, GET /createUser?name=Jo, PUT /updateAllUsers

Original Endpoints

Provided endpoints (as given):
  • GET
    /getUsers
    Nonstandard naming; likely returns multiple users.
  • POST
    /user/delete/5
    Deletes user id=5 — uses POST and includes action in path.
  • GET
    /createUser?name=Jo
    Creates a user via GET with query parameter — unsafe and violates idempotency.
  • PUT
    /updateAllUsers
    Unclear scope: updates all users? Non-resourceful naming.

Identified Design Problems

Each problem is listed with a short explanation and why it matters.
Problem: Verb/action names in URI (e.g., /getUsers, /createUser, /updateAllUsers). URIs should represent resources, not actions.
Problem: Incorrect HTTP methods:
  • Using GET for state-changing create operation (/createUser).
  • Using POST for delete (/user/delete/5) instead of DELETE.
Problem: Mixing actions inside path segments (e.g., /user/delete/5). This leaks intent into the URI and breaks predictability.
Problem: Inconsistent pluralization and resource naming (user vs users). Use plural nouns for collections: /users.
Problem: Identifier placement and semantics — id should be a resource identifier: /users/{id}.
Problem: Query parameters used to create resources (GET /createUser?name=Jo) — unsafe, non-idempotent use of GET.
Problem: Overbroad endpoint /updateAllUsers — ambiguous whether it's bulk update or misnamed single update; lacks clear request body/schema.
Problem: No standard response status codes described — clients won't reliably interpret results.
Problem: No request/response schema, authentication, pagination, filtering, or error formats documented.
Summary fix strategy:
  • Use nouns for resources (e.g., /users).
  • Use correct HTTP verbs: GET (read), POST (create), PUT/PATCH (update), DELETE (delete).
  • Place IDs as path parameters: /users/{id}.
  • Use GET only for safe, idempotent reads; use POST for create with body payload.
  • Document status codes, request/response schemas, pagination, and errors consistently (prefer JSON).

Corrected RESTful Endpoints

Below are recommended endpoints with methods, purpose, expected inputs, and typical response status codes. Click each to expand examples and sample payloads.
GET /users — List users
GET
/users
Returns a paginated list of users. Safe and idempotent.
200 OK
Query params (optional): page, per_page, sort, q (search), filter.*
{
  "page": 1,
  "per_page": 20,
  "total": 124,
  "data": [
    { "id": 5, "name": "Jo", "email": "jo@example.com" },
    { "id": 6, "name": "Ana", "email": "ana@example.com" }
  ]
}
GET /users/{id} — Retrieve single user
GET
/users/{id}
Fetch a single user by id.
200 OK / 404 Not Found
{
  "id": 5,
  "name": "Jo",
  "email": "jo@example.com"
}
POST /users — Create a user
POST
/users
Create a user. Request body contains stable representation of the new resource.
201 Created / 400 Bad Request
Request (application/json)
{
  "name": "Jo",
  "email": "jo@example.com",
  "password": "s3cr3t"  // send hashed over TLS; avoid returning password
}
Response (201 Created) with Location header
HTTP/1.1 201 Created
Location: /users/123
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "id": 123,
  "name": "Jo",
  "email": "jo@example.com"
}
PATCH /users/{id} — Partially update a user (preferred)
PATCH
/users/{id}
Partially update fields. Use PUT for full replace, PATCH for partial updates.
200 OK / 204 No Content / 400 / 404
Request (example):
{
  "name": "Joanna"
}
Response (200 OK)
{
  "id": 5,
  "name": "Joanna",
  "email": "jo@example.com"
}
DELETE /users/{id} — Delete a user
DELETE
/users/{id}
Delete the user resource identified by id.
204 No Content / 404 Not Found
Response (successful):
HTTP/1.1 204 No Content
Bulk operations (if needed) — POST /users:bulk, PATCH /users (filtered)
When performing bulk updates or deletes, use explicit endpoints and request bodies rather than action names in path.
POST
/users:bulk-delete
Bulk delete by ids in request body (or use POST /users/bulk-delete). Keep clear naming and document behavior.
200 OK / 207 Multi-Status
{
  "ids": [5, 9, 12]
}
Alternatively, use PATCH with filters:
PATCH /users
{
  "filter": { "inactive": true },
  "update": { "status": "archived" }
}

Status Codes, Errors & Best Practices

Consistent responses help clients handle results correctly.
  • 200 OK — successful GET, PATCH returning resource.
  • 201 Created — successful POST creating resource; include Location header.
  • 204 No Content — successful DELETE or successful PUT/PATCH with no body.
  • 400 Bad Request — validation errors; return an errors array.
  • 401 Unauthorized — missing/invalid auth credentials.
  • 403 Forbidden — authenticated but not allowed.
  • 404 Not Found — resource does not exist.
  • 409 Conflict — duplicate resource or version conflict.
  • 429 Too Many Requests — rate limiting.
  • 5xx — server errors.
Error response example (400):
{
  "status": 400,
  "error": "Bad Request",
  "message": "Email is required",
  "errors": [
    { "field": "email", "message": "Email must be a valid address" }
  ],
  "timestamp": "2026-03-08T12:34:56Z"
}

Naming & Modeling Guidelines

Short checklist when designing endpoints
  • Use plural nouns for collections: /users, /invoices
  • Use path parameters for resource ids: /users/{id}
  • Use query parameters for filtering, pagination, and searching
  • Prefer PATCH for partial updates; PUT for full replace
  • Keep URIs stable; use versioning (e.g., /v1/users) for breaking changes
  • Return consistent error structure and use meaningful HTTP status codes
  • Document required authentication (Bearer token, OAuth2, etc.) and scopes

Quick Reference

Mapping original → recommended
GET /getUsers
→ /users (GET)
POST /user/delete/5
→ /users/5 (DELETE)
GET /createUser?name=Jo
→ /users (POST with JSON body)
PUT /updateAllUsers
→ Evaluate intent: PATCH /users (bulk) or PATCH /users/{id}
Minimal response patterns
Create: 201 + Location
Read: 200 + body
Update: 200/204
Delete: 204
Security & headers
  • Authorization: Bearer <token>
  • Accept: application/json
  • Content-Type: application/json
  • Use TLS (HTTPS) always
Click to copy a canonical create-user payload to clipboard.

Useful Tips

  1. Version your API in the path or header (/v1/users).
  2. Prefer snake_case or camelCase consistently in JSON — pick one.
  3. Support pagination & limits to avoid returning large lists.
  4. Consider ETags or If-Match for safe concurrent updates.
Need changes tailored to your app? Provide expected behaviors, auth requirements, and sample data and I’ll produce a complete OpenAPI/Swagger document to match.

responses.text:
Users API

User Management — Interactive API Reference

Examines the original proposed endpoints, explains every design problem, and presents corrected RESTful endpoints with examples, status codes, and an interactive tester (simulated).

Original Proposed Endpoints As provided by the developer
  • GET /getUsers
  • POST /user/delete/5
  • GET /createUser?name=Jo
  • PUT /updateAllUsers
Design problems (comprehensive)

Every issue in the original endpoints, with short explanation and impact.

1) Verb in the path (GET /getUsers)
Paths should represent resources (nouns). Using the HTTP method and a verb in the path duplicates intent and breaks consistency. Use /users with GET instead.
2) Using POST for deletion and embedding action + id in path (POST /user/delete/5)
DELETE is the correct HTTP verb for resource deletion. Use /users/{id} rather than /user/delete/{id}. Embedding action in path mixes RPC style into REST.
3) Creating resources with GET and query parameters (GET /createUser?name=Jo)
GET must be safe and idempotent and not change server state. Use POST to /users with the new resource in the request body. Query strings are poor for structured payloads and sensitive data.
4) Ambiguous mass-update endpoint and method choice (PUT /updateAllUsers)
PUT implies a full replace of a resource; an endpoint named updateAllUsers suggests a batch operation. Define precise semantics: if replacing entire collection, PUT /users with collection payload; for partial changes, PATCH /users or PATCH /users/{id}. Avoid vague names.
5) Inconsistent resource naming (/user vs /users)
Use plural nouns for collections (/users). Mixing singular and plural confuses clients and makes documentation unclear.
6) Missing content type and request/response bodies
Endpoints don't specify JSON payload shape, required fields, or content-type (application/json). Good API docs must list request/response schemas and examples.
7) No mention of status codes, error responses, or validation
Clients need consistent use of HTTP status codes (200, 201, 204, 400, 404, 409, 500) and example error bodies.
8) Lack of versioning and authentication notes
APIs should be versioned (e.g., /v1/users). Also indicate needed auth (Bearer token, scopes) and rate limits.
9) Poor discoverability and no HATEOAS or links
While optional, including link relations or clear resource URIs helps clients navigate (e.g., Location header on create, self links in responses).
10) No pagination, filtering, or sorting semantics for list endpoints
GET /users should support pagination (limit, offset or cursor), filtering, and sorting to avoid large payloads and unclear behavior.
11) Unclear id format and URL encoding
Define whether IDs are numeric, UUIDs, slugs; ensure clients know to URL-encode path segments.
12) No examples for successful or failed operations
Examples reduce integration errors and speed up adoption—include sample requests and responses for success and error cases.
13) Using verbs in endpoint names reduces cacheability and intermediary behavior
Standard HTTP semantics enable caching (GET), idempotency (PUT, DELETE), and safe operations. Custom verbs obscure this.
Corrected RESTful API — Summary

Below are recommended endpoints, HTTP methods, expected status codes, and short notes. Base path includes versioning: /api/v1

GET
/api/v1/users
List users — supports pagination, filtering, fields, sorting
200 OK — returns { data: [User], meta: {pagination} }
POST
/api/v1/users
Create a user. Body: application/json with user object.
201 Created — Location header and created resource in body; 400 Bad Request on validation error
GET
/api/v1/users/{id}
Retrieve single user by id
200 OK or 404 Not Found
PATCH
/api/v1/users/{id}
Partial update of a user (preferred for updates of some fields)
200 OK (with updated resource) or 204 No Content; 400/404 as appropriate
PUT
/api/v1/users/{id}
Full replace of user resource. Use only if you accept full-object semantics
200 OK or 204 No Content; 400/404 as appropriate
DELETE
/api/v1/users/{id}
Delete user
204 No Content on success; 404 if not found
Batch operations (examples)
- Bulk create: POST /api/v1/users/batch (body: array of users) — 201 Created with results
- Bulk update: PATCH /api/v1/users (body: {ids:[], changes:{...}}) — 200 OK with per-item status
- Bulk delete: DELETE /api/v1/users (body: {ids:[...]}) or POST /api/v1/users:delete with caution — prefer DELETE with body or POST to a subresource /batch-delete if DELETE with body unsupported by clients.
Schemas and examples

Define concise request/response shapes. Use application/json.

User (response)
{
  "id": "uuid-or-int",
  "name": "Jo",
  "email": "jo@example.com",
  "createdAt": "2025-08-10T12:34:56Z",
  "updatedAt": "2025-08-10T12:34:56Z"
}
Create user (request)
POST /api/v1/users
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "name": "Jo",
  "email": "jo@example.com",
  "password": "securePassword123"
}
Create response
201 Created
Location: /api/v1/users/123

{
  "data": {
    "id":"123",
    "name":"Jo",
    "email":"jo@example.com",
    "createdAt":"2025-08-10T12:34:56Z"
  }
}
Error (validation)
400 Bad Request

{
  "error": {
    "code": "INVALID_REQUEST",
    "message": "Email is required",
    "fields": { "email": "required" }
  }
}
Interactive examples (simulated)

This tester simulates requests and shows expected responses/formats. It does not call a live backend.

GET /api/v1/users
POST /api/v1/users
DELETE /api/v1/users/{id}
Status code guidance
  • 200 OK — Successful GET/ PATCH (returns body)
  • 201 Created — Successful POST creating resource (Location header)
  • 204 No Content — Successful DELETE or PUT with no response body
  • 400 Bad Request — Validation or malformed JSON
  • 401 Unauthorized — Missing/invalid auth
  • 403 Forbidden — Authenticated but not allowed
  • 404 Not Found — Resource not found
  • 409 Conflict — Conflict (e.g., duplicate unique field)
  • 500 Internal Server Error — Unexpected server error
Notes & best practices
  • Version your API in the URL (/v1) or headers.
  • Use JSON and include Content-Type and Accept headers.
  • Prefer PATCH for partial updates; PUT for full replace.
  • Return consistent envelope (e.g., {data, meta, error}).
  • Document authentication, rate limits, and id formats.
  • Provide examples for success and error scenarios.
Interactive doc generated. The simulation below demonstrates expected request/response format (mocked).

API

User Management API — Interactive Reference

Clean, consistent REST endpoints, clear status codes, and examples. Click an operation to expand details and try the example (simulated).

Original endpoints provided
GET /getUsers
POST /user/delete/5
GET /createUser?name=Jo
PUT /updateAllUsers
Issues & fixes

Design problems (all issues listed)

Naming Endpoint paths include verbs (get, create, delete, update) rather than resources. REST favors nouns/resources (e.g., /users).
HTTP method misuse GET is used for creation (/createUser), and POST for delete (/user/delete/5). Methods should reflect intent: GET=read, POST=create, PUT/PATCH=update, DELETE=delete.
Inconsistent path conventions Mixed singular/plural (/user vs /getUsers), camelCase verbs in path, inconsistent ordering. Use consistent plural resource names (e.g., /users).
Resource addressing Using id in path for delete is ok, but it's embedded in a verb path (/user/delete/5) rather than /users/5. Prefer /users/{id}.
Query misuse Passing "name" in query to create a resource (GET /createUser?name=Jo) violates safe semantics of GET and cached endpoints. Use POST with JSON body or form data.
Ambiguous update PUT /updateAllUsers is unclear: does it bulk update? Replace all users? Lacks path and body contract. Use /users/{id} (PUT/PATCH) or /users/bulk with clear semantics.
Status code expectations No guidance on status codes. Each operation must document expected codes (200/201/204/400/404/422/500).
Security & filtering No mention of authentication, pagination, filtering, validation, rate limits. Real APIs must support these (e.g., Authorization header, ?:page & size).
Idempotency Unclear idempotency. POST should create and be non-idempotent; PUT should be idempotent. Current endpoints mix responsibilities.
Response shape No definition of response bodies, error formats, or content-types. Prefer JSON:API or a simple consistent structure with error objects.
HTTP caching Using GET for operations with side effects breaks caching semantics. Also no cache headers documented.
Hypermedia & discoverability Endpoints don't include links or consistent structure (optional but helpful).
Pluralization & language Mixed case and inconsistent grammar reduces clarity for third-party developers.

Corrected endpoints (concise)

GET
/users
List users (supports pagination, filtering)
POST
/users
Create a user
GET
/users/{id}
Get a single user
PATCH
/users/{id}
Partially update a user
PUT
/users/{id}
Replace a user (idempotent)
DELETE
/users/{id}
Delete a user
POST
/users:bulk-update
Bulk operations (clear contract)
Notes: Use Authorization: Bearer & JSON bodies. Use standard HTTP status codes. Prefer PATCH for partial updates and PUT for full replacements. Bulk endpoints should be explicit and guarded.
Interactive operation details

Select an operation

Simulated "Try it" — no network calls are made
Choose an operation on the left to view its full contract: method, path, request/response examples, status codes, and a simulated try-it form with validation.

prompt_text: Review these badly designed REST endpoints for a user management system: GET /getUsers, POST /user/delete/5, GET /createUser?name=Jo, PUT /updateAllUsers. Create a single self-contained HTML file that serves as an interactive API documentation page. Identify every single design problem in the original endpoints, then show the corrected versions following REST best practices with proper HTTP methods, consistent naming, and status codes. Use expandable sections.

responses.text:
API Review & Corrected Design — User Management

Interactive, self-contained documentation that (1) lists every design problem in the original endpoints and (2) shows corrected RESTful endpoints, methods, responses and examples.

REST best practices
consistent resource names • proper HTTP verbs • status codes • clear URIs
Original Endpoints (bad designs) Provided for review
List
GET
/getUsers
Notes: non-standard noun, redundant "get"
POST
/user/delete/5
Notes: action in path, using POST instead of DELETE, id in path but also verb present
GET
/createUser?name=Jo
Notes: action in path, creation via GET, query param for body data
PUT
/updateAllUsers
Notes: action in path, unclear what "all" means, idempotency concerns
Every single design problem (annotated) Click each problem to expand examples and recommended fix
Problems common to several endpoints
1) Action verbs in URI paths
Examples: /getUsers, /createUser, /updateAllUsers, /user/delete/5
Why it's bad: REST resources should be nouns (representing collections/resources). Actions belong to HTTP methods. Including verbs causes inconsistent design, confuses caching and tooling, and hides intent.
2) Wrong HTTP methods
Examples: creation via GET (/createUser), deletion via POST (/user/delete/5)
Why it's bad: GET should be safe and idempotent (no side effects). Use POST to create, DELETE to remove, PUT/PATCH to update. Wrong verbs break proxies, caching, and client expectations.
3) Inconsistent resource naming and casing
Examples: /getUsers (camelCase), /user/delete/5 (singular 'user'), /createUser (camelCase)
Why it's bad: APIs should use consistent, predictable URI casing and pluralization (commonly lowercase, hyphenated or simple plural nouns: /users).
4) Using query parameters for resource creation/identifying entities
Example: /createUser?name=Jo
Why it's bad: GET with query parameters may be cached and should not cause resource creation. Creation payloads belong in request bodies (usually JSON) with POST.
5) Unclear, overloaded endpoints
Example: /updateAllUsers — does it update every user? Which fields? Partial vs full update?
Why it's bad: Endpoints should be specific. If updating a collection, document the behavior and use appropriate HTTP semantics (PUT for replacing a resource, PATCH for partial updates).
6) Status codes and responses not specified
Original endpoints didn't state success/failure codes, error payloads, or validation rules.
Why it's bad: Clients need deterministic HTTP status codes (200/201/204/400/404/409/500 etc.) and consistent response shapes (usually JSON with error metadata).
7) Lack of resource identifiers and resource hierarchy
Example: mixing singular and plural, embedding verbs with ids (/user/delete/5)
Why it's bad: Use hierarchical resources like /users/{id}/... and avoid embedding verbs between resource and id.
8) Idempotency not considered
E.g., using POST to delete may be non-idempotent when it should be idempotent (DELETE is idempotent).
Why it's bad: Idempotency affects retry logic. Choose semantics accordingly.
Endpoint-specific problems (expand each)
/getUsers — problems
  • "get" is a verb; path should be a noun like /users.
  • Not clear whether it returns all users or supports pagination/filtering.
  • No mention of status codes (200, 204 if empty) or pagination headers.
Fix
Use GET /users, support query parameters for filtering/pagination, return 200 with JSON list and pagination metadata or 204 when nothing to return (or 200 with empty array).
/user/delete/5 — problems
  • Action "delete" in path; wrong HTTP verb (POST).
  • Singular "user" mixed with numeric id and action.
  • Ambiguous response: should return 204 if deleted, 404 if not found.
Fix
Use DELETE /users/5, return 204 No Content on success, 404 Not Found if user doesn't exist. For soft deletes, return 200 with representation.
/createUser?name=Jo — problems
  • GET used for creation (side effects) — violates safe semantics.
  • Query parameter for body data — limited and insecure; no validation shown.
  • No content-type/accept headers specified.
Fix
Use POST /users with JSON body, e.g. { "name": "Jo" }, return 201 Created with Location header /users/{id} and the created user in body (or 201 with minimal body).
/updateAllUsers — problems
  • Unclear intent: replace all users? apply patch to all? which fields?
  • PUT vs PATCH confusion: PUT should replace resource, PATCH for partial updates.
  • Potentially dangerous bulk operation without authentication/authorization checks or idempotency guarantees.
Fix
If you mean update multiple users partially, use PATCH /users with a well-defined bulk update request format. If replacing entire collection, use PUT /users with full collection representation (rare and risky). Provide clear responses and run jobs asynchronously for large operations.
Corrected API design (RESTful) Consistent resource naming, methods, and status codes
Overview — conventions used in corrected design
- Resource base: /users (plural, lowercase).
- Use HTTP methods: GET (read), POST (create), PUT (replace), PATCH (partial update), DELETE (delete).
- Use JSON for request/response bodies (Content-Type: application/json).
- Return appropriate status codes: 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content, 400 Bad Request, 404 Not Found, 409 Conflict, 422 Unprocessable Entity, 500 Server Error.
- Support pagination via query params: ?page=1&limit=25 or Cursor-based. Include Link headers or pagination object.
- Document error response shape: { "error": { "code": "string", "message":"string", "details": {...} } }.
Corrected endpoints
GET
/users
List users. Supports filtering, searching, pagination.
GET /users?page=1&limit=20&search=jo HTTP/1.1 Accept: application/json Response 200 OK { "meta": { "page": 1, "limit": 20, "total": 124 }, "data": [ { "id": 5, "name": "Jo", "email": "jo@example.com" }, ... ] }
POST
/users
Create a new user. Body contains attributes. Returns 201 Created, Location header set.
POST /users HTTP/1.1 Content-Type: application/json Accept: application/json { "name": "Jo", "email": "jo@example.com" } Response 201 Created Location: /users/42 { "id": 42, "name": "Jo", "email": "jo@example.com", "createdAt": "2026-01-20T12:34:56Z" }
GET
/users/{id}
Retrieve a single user by ID.
GET /users/42 HTTP/1.1 Accept: application/json Response 200 OK { "id": 42, "name": "Jo", "email": "jo@example.com" } Response 404 Not Found { "error": { "code": "user_not_found", "message": "User 42 not found" } }
PUT
/users/{id}
Replace a user completely. Provide all fields. Idempotent. Returns 200 or 204.
PUT /users/42 HTTP/1.1 Content-Type: application/json { "name": "Joanne", "email": "joanne@example.com" } Response 200 OK { "id": 42, "name": "Joanne", "email": "joanne@example.com" }
PATCH
/users/{id}
Partial update (preferred for most updates). Returns 200 with updated resource.
PATCH /users/42 HTTP/1.1 Content-Type: application/json { "name": "Jo" } Response 200 OK { "id": 42, "name": "Jo", "email": "jo@example.com" }
DELETE
/users/{id}
Delete a user. Return 204 No Content for hard delete, or 200 with the updated (soft-deleted) representation.
DELETE /users/42 HTTP/1.1 Response 204 No Content If not found: Response 404 Not Found { "error": { "code": "user_not_found", "message": "User 42 not found" } }
Bulk updates / collection operations
If you need to update many users in one request: - Use PATCH /users with a defined bulk operation payload, or - Use a job endpoint: POST /users/bulk-updates -> 202 Accepted with Location to job status.
PATCH /users HTTP/1.1 Content-Type: application/json { "filter": { "status": "inactive" }, "patch": { "emailOptIn": false } } Response 202 Accepted Location: /jobs/12345 { "jobId": "12345", "status": "processing" }
Status codes reference (recommended) Use these consistently
200 OK — successful read/modify returning body
201 Created — resource created, Location header points to it
202 Accepted — request accepted for async processing
204 No Content — successful action with no body (e.g., DELETE)
400 Bad Request — validation/client error
401 Unauthorized — missing/invalid auth
403 Forbidden — authenticated but not allowed
404 Not Found — resource missing
409 Conflict — e.g., duplicate unique field
422 Unprocessable Entity — semantic validation failed
500 Server Error — unexpected error
Example error response format Consistent error payload
HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request Content-Type: application/json { "error": { "code": "invalid_input", "message": "Email is missing or invalid", "details": { "field": "email", "reason": "required" } } }
Migration notes: mapping old -> new How to transition safely
Recommended mapping and compatibility
- GET /getUsers -> GET /users. Add redirects (301/308) or support both paths temporarily returning 200 with identical payload. Prefer redirect or deprecation header: Deprecated: true and Link to new URI.
- POST /user/delete/5 -> DELETE /users/5. Support POST for a short window but emit deprecation warnings and logs.
- GET /createUser?name=Jo -> POST /users with JSON. Reject GET-create with 405 Method Not Allowed if you can.
- PUT /updateAllUsers -> either PATCH /users or POST /users/bulk-updates depending on desired behavior. Introduce async job pattern for long-running bulk ops.
- Add OpenAPI contract and version your API (e.g., /v1/users) to allow safe evolution.
Interactive examples (try copy) Copy curl snippets to clipboard
curl -i -H "Accept: application/json" "GET https://api.example.com/users?page=1&limit=10"
curl -i -X POST "https://api.example.com/users" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"name":"Jo","email":"jo@example.com"}'
Last updated: 2026-03-08 — Summary: Replace verbful paths with resource-based URIs (use plural nouns), map actions to HTTP verbs, return proper status codes, and document error formats and pagination. For bulk ops prefer PATCH /users or job endpoints (POST /users/bulk-updates) and clearly document semantics.

User API — Interactive Fix & Docs

This page analyzes a set of badly designed endpoints, lists every design problem, and presents corrected RESTful endpoints, sample requests/responses, and status-code guidance. Use the interactive demos to experiment with example payloads and see expected responses.

Original (bad) endpoints

Provided endpoints to review:
  • GET /getUsers
  • POST /user/delete/5
  • GET /createUser?name=Jo
  • PUT /updateAllUsers
All design problems (full point-by-point list)

Each problem below applies to one or more of the original endpoints. Where relevant, the specific endpoints are listed in parentheses.

1) Verb in URL path
Paths like /getUsers, /createUser, /updateAllUsers, and /user/delete/5 include HTTP verbs or actions. RESTful URLs should model resources (nouns), not actions; the HTTP method already conveys the action.
2) Inconsistent resource naming and pluralization
Both /getUsers and /user/delete/5 use different singular/plural forms. Use consistent plural resource names (typically /users).
3) Misuse of HTTP methods
Examples:
  • Using GET to create a resource (GET /createUser?name=Jo) — GET must be safe and idempotent and not modify state.
  • Using POST to delete (POST /user/delete/5) instead of DELETE.
  • Using PUT with ambiguous target (PUT /updateAllUsers) without a clear resource or idempotency meaning.
4) Poor use of path parameters vs query parameters
Using a path segment for the ID but also embedding an action (/user/delete/5) is wrong. IDs should be in the resource path (/users/5). Query parameters should be for filters, pagination, sorting, or optional modifiers — not for required resource properties for creation.
5) Side effects in GET requests
GET must be safe (no state change) and cacheable. GET /createUser?name=Jo performs a create, which breaks caching and intermediaries and confuses clients.
6) Ambiguous or implicit request/response bodies and parameters
No clear specification of how to pass user data (JSON body vs query). For example, creation should use JSON in the request body with a media type like application/json.
7) Inappropriate or missing status-code guidance
No mention of expected HTTP status codes (201 Created, 204 No Content, 200 OK, 404 Not Found, 400 Bad Request, 409 Conflict, etc.) and where to include Location headers on creation.
8) Lack of pagination, filtering, and security considerations
A naive "get all" endpoint should support pagination and filtering. Also missing authentication/authorization hints (e.g., 401/403) and input validation guidance (400).
9) Missing content negotiation and media type
No mention of Content-Type or Accept headers. Standardize on JSON (application/json) or allow content negotiation.
10) Bulk operation semantics unclear
PUT /updateAllUsers is vague: is it replacing all users (huge risk), applying a patch to all, or performing batch updates? Define endpoints for bulk operations and explain atomicity/partial-failure behavior.
11) Endpoint discoverability and RESTful HATEOAS missing
No links or hypermedia are described. While HATEOAS is optional in many APIs, documenting relationships and standard resource URLs improves discoverability.
12) Path hierarchy issues
Use versioning and hierarchical paths (e.g., /api/v1/users) to allow future changes without breaking clients.
That covers all design problems present or implied by the original endpoints. Next: corrected endpoints and examples.

Corrected endpoints (REST best practices)

Use the following resource-oriented endpoints. Base path includes versioning: /api/v1. All request/response bodies use JSON unless stated otherwise.
Resource collection and item endpoints
GET
/api/v1/users
List users (supports pagination, filtering, sorting)
  • Query params: page, per_page, q (search), sort, role, etc.
  • Responses:
    • 200 OK — body: { data: [user], meta: {pagination...} }
    • 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden
POST
/api/v1/users
Create a new user
  • Request: 201 Created — return Location header pointing to /api/v1/users/{id} and response body with the created user.
  • Validation errors: 400 Bad Request. Conflict (duplicate): 409 Conflict.
  • Example request body: { "name":"Jo", "email":"jo@example.com" }
GET
/api/v1/users/{id}
Retrieve a single user
  • Responses: 200 OK, 404 Not Found, 401/403 as appropriate.
PUT
/api/v1/users/{id}
Replace a user's representation (idempotent). Provide full user object.
  • Responses: 200 OK (with updated resource) or 204 No Content; 400 for invalid payload; 404 if missing.
PATCH
/api/v1/users/{id}
Partial update (patch). Prefer JSON Patch (application/json-patch+json) or a partial JSON object.
  • Responses: 200 OK or 204 No Content; 400, 404.
DELETE
/api/v1/users/{id}
Delete a user by ID
  • Responses: 204 No Content on success; 404 Not Found if missing; 401/403 for auth errors.
Bulk operations and batch endpoints

Design bulk endpoints explicitly and clearly document behavior (atomic vs partial). Examples:

  • PUT /api/v1/users — Replace whole collection (rare, dangerous). Use with caution and explicit semantics.

REST API Review & Corrections — Interactive Guide

REST API Review & Corrections — Interactive Guide

Analyzes the supplied endpoints, points out every problem, and provides corrected, consistent RESTful designs with examples and recommended HTTP semantics.

Original endpoints (as provided)

GET  /getUsers
POST /user/delete/5
GET  /createUser?name=Jo
PUT  /updateAllUsers

High-level problems found (summary)

  • Inconsistent naming and casing (getUsers, user, createUser, updateAllUsers).
  • Actions (get, create, delete, update) are embedded in paths rather than using HTTP methods and resources.
  • Using GET for state-changing operations (e.g., creating resources) — violates safe/idempotent semantics.
  • Using POST with a delete verb in the path (/user/delete/5) instead of DELETE method on the resource URI.
  • Using query strings to create a resource (GET /createUser?name=Jo) — wrong method and wrong place for payload.
  • Ambiguous resource identifiers and pluralization inconsistencies (user vs getUsers vs updateAllUsers).
  • No clear request/response bodies, status codes, pagination, filtering, or error conventions documented.

Design principles used to correct the API

  • Use nouns (resources) in URIs, not verbs. Example: /users, /users/{id}.
  • Use HTTP methods for intent:
    • GET — retrieve (safe, idempotent)
    • POST — create (not necessarily idempotent)
    • PUT — replace a resource (idempotent)
    • PATCH — partially update a resource (idempotent)
    • DELETE — remove a resource (idempotent)
  • Use plural resource names for collections (e.g., /users).
  • PUT to a collection should be avoided for partial updates; use PATCH for partial updates to a resource. To replace an entire collection, use an explicit URI and careful semantics.
  • Use request bodies for create/update payloads (JSON). Use query parameters for filtering, sorting, pagination.
  • Return proper HTTP status codes and consistent error objects (e.g., 200/201/204/400/404/409/500).
  • Design for hypermedia where practical (HATEOAS optional). Keep URIs discoverable and predictable.

Endpoint-by-endpoint analysis and corrections

Endpoint: GET /getUsers

Problems identified

  • URI contains a verb "get" — path should represent a resource (noun).
  • Likely should be /users (collection). Using verbs in paths is redundant with HTTP methods.
  • No pagination/filtering options shown; returning entire dataset could be inefficient.
  • No response format, error handling, or status code guidance.

Corrected design

Resource GET /users

Description: Retrieve a paginated list of users. Safe and idempotent.

GET /users?page=1&per_page=25&sort=created_at:desc&search=jo

Headers: 

Accept: application/json

Success response (200 OK):

{
  "data": [
    { "id": 5, "name": "Jo", "email": "jo@example.com", "created_at":"2025-11-02T12:34:56Z" },
    ...
  ],
  "meta": { "page": 1, "per_page": 25, "total": 347 },
  "links": { "self": "/users?page=1&per_page=25", "next": "/users?page=2&per_page=25" }
}

Notes:

  • Use standard query parameters for pagination (page/per_page or limit/offset).
  • Provide filtering (e.g., ?name=Jo or ?search=jo), sorting, and field selection (?fields=id,name).
  • Consider caching headers (ETag, Cache-Control) for GET responses.

Endpoint: POST /user/delete/5

Problems identified

  • Embedding an action "delete" in the path while using POST is incorrect. DELETE is the appropriate HTTP method.
  • Path uses singular /user while the rest use other forms — inconsistent. Prefer plurals for collections: /users/5.
  • POST is not idempotent while DELETE should be idempotent; using wrong method changes semantics and can confuse clients.

Corrected design

Resource DELETE /users/{id}

Description: Delete user with id 5.

DELETE /users/5

Success responses:

  • 204 No Content — if deletion succeeded and there's no body to return.
  • 200 OK — if you return a body describing the deleted resource or a deletion confirmation.

Error responses:

  • 404 Not Found — if user 5 doesn't exist.
  • 403 Forbidden — if client not allowed to delete.

If you need to support soft deletes or bulk deletes:

  • Soft delete: PATCH /users/5 with body { "deleted": true } or a dedicated action endpoint like POST /users/5/deactivate (but prefer PATCH/PUT to use HTTP semantics).
  • Bulk delete: POST /users/delete with a JSON body { "ids": [5,8,9] } or better: DELETE /users?ids=5,8,9 (note: not all servers/clients like bodies on DELETE; use POST /users/bulk-delete if you need a request body and the operation is not idempotent).
Endpoint: GET /createUser?name=Jo

Problems identified

  • GET is used to create state — violates safe semantics of GET. GET must not change server state.
  • Using query string to carry create payload is nonstandard and limited (length limits, poor structure for complex objects).
  • Path contains a verb "createUser". Resource URIs should be nouns like /users.

Corrected design

Resource POST /users

Description: Create a new user.

POST /users
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "name": "Jo",
  "email": "jo@example.com",
  "password": "secret"    // or omitted if created via OAuth/SSO
}

Success response:

  • 201 Created — Location header set to the new resource URI: 
    Location: /users/42
    Response body (optional): 
    {
  "id": 42,
  "name": "Jo",
  "email": "jo@example.com",
  "created_at": "2026-03-01T15:12:00Z"
}

Notes:

  • Use server-side validation; return 400 Bad Request with an error object if validation fails.
  • If creation can result in a conflict (duplicate email), return 409 Conflict with details.
  • Do not pass secrets (passwords) in query strings or via GET.

Endpoint: PUT /updateAllUsers

Problems identified

  • Path includes a verb "updateAllUsers"; resource URIs should be nouns. "All users" is ambiguous.
  • PUT semantics are for replacing a resource at a URI. PUTting to a verb-like collection name is confusing.
  • It's unclear whether this is a bulk update, a replace of the entire collection, or something else.

Corrected designs (choose based on intent)

If you want to replace the entire users collection (rare):

PUT /users
Content-Type: application/json

[
  { "id": 1, "name": "Alice", ... },
  { "id": 2, "name": "Bob", ... }
]

Notes: Replacing entire collections is rarely desirable — it's risky, large, and concurrency-prone. This operation should be used only with care, strong auth, and transactional semantics.

If you want to perform a bulk partial update (most common):

PATCH /users
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "filter": { "active": false, "country": "US" },
  "patch": { "marketing_opt_in": false }
}

Notes:

  • This is a bulk operation that updates multiple resources matching the filter.
  • It should be documented as non-idempotent or idempotent depending on implementation; choose method accordingly (PATCH is appropriate for partial updates).
  • Consider background job semantics: respond 202 Accepted with a job resource that can be polled: POST /jobs with body describing the bulk update.

If you want to update a single user:

PATCH /users/{id}
Content-Type: application/json

{ "name": "Josephine" }

Recommended pattern for long-running/bulk tasks:

POST /bulk/user-updates
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "filter": { "signup_before": "2020-01-01" },
  "update": { "tier": "legacy", "email_verified": true }
}

Response: 202 Accepted
Location: /jobs/678
Body: { "job_id": 678, "status": "pending" }

Consistent, corrected API reference (final suggestion)

Use these endpoints as a minimal, consistent user-management API.

GET /users
List users (paginated, filterable)
GET /users?page=1&per_page=25&search=jo&country=US
200 OK — returns collection with meta/links
POST /users
Create a new user
POST /users
Content-Type: application/json

{ "name":"Jo", "email":"jo@example.com", "password":"…" }
201 Created — Location: /users/{id}
GET /users/{id}
Retrieve single user
GET /users/5
200 OK or 404 Not Found
PATCH /users/{id}
Partially update a user
PATCH /users/5
Content-Type: application/json

{ "name": "Josephine" }
200 OK (or 204 No Content)
DELETE /users/{id}
Delete a user
DELETE /users/5
204 No Content or 200 OK
POST /users/bulk-update
Bulk update — recommended to return 202 + job resource
POST /users/bulk-update
Content-Type: application/json

{ "filter": { "country":"US" }, "update": { "marketing_opt_in": false } }
202 Accepted — Location: /jobs/{id}
Error and status conventions
  • 400 Bad Request — validation errors. Return { "error": "validation_failed", "details": {...} }
  • 401 Unauthorized — missing/invalid auth.
  • 403 Forbidden — not allowed.
  • 404 Not Found — resource doesn't exist.
  • 409 Conflict — resource conflict (duplicate email).
  • 429 Too Many Requests — rate limits.
  • 500/502/503 — server errors.
Security & other recommendations
  • Always use HTTPS. Never pass secrets in URLs or query strings.
  • Use standard authentication (Bearer tokens / OAuth2 / API keys) and return 401 for missing/invalid tokens.
  • Document rate limits and pagination defaults.
  • Consider HATEOAS links in responses for discoverability (optional).

Interactive REST API Review & Corrections

You provided these endpoints: GET /getUsers, POST /user/delete/5, GET /createUser?name=Jo, PUT /updateAllUsers

Overview — what's wrong (high level)

  • X Inconsistent URL naming and verbs-in-path: endpoints include actions (get/create/delete/update) in the path and mix singular/plural forms. REST favors resource-based URLs (nouns) and uses HTTP methods to express actions.
  • X Incorrect use of HTTP methods: GET used to create, POST used to delete; these violate idempotency and semantics (and can break caching and intermediaries).
  • X Non-RESTful parameter placement: using path for an action verb and an ID, or query string for creation data; bodies should carry complex data when appropriate.
  • X Ambiguous endpoints (e.g., updateAllUsers) lacking clear resource identification and intent for PUT vs PATCH, and unclear request/response shapes.
  • X No status code guidance, no content types, no error formats, and no pagination/filtering guidance for list endpoints.

Correct, consistent design — principles used

  1. Resources are nouns: use plural resource names (e.g., /users), not verbs in the path.
  2. Use HTTP methods for intent:
    • GET — read (safe, usually cacheable); list or retrieve resources.
    • POST — create a subordinate resource (non-idempotent).
    • PUT — replace a resource (idempotent).
    • PATCH — partial update (ideally using a patch format).
    • DELETE — delete a resource (idempotent).
  3. Use path parameters for resource identity (e.g., /users/{id}), and use query parameters for filtering, paging, sorting.
  4. Use JSON request/response bodies and standard HTTP status codes (200/201/204/400/404/409/500, etc.).
  5. Support pagination for lists and provide consistent error response structure (e.g., problem+json or a simple {error, message, code}).

Endpoint-by-endpoint analysis and corrections

OriginalProblemsCorrected design
GET /getUsers
  • Path contains verb "get".
  • No pagination/filtering indicated — returns potentially large lists.
  • Unclear response shape and cache rules.
✓ Use GET /users
Details:
  • GET /users — returns a paginated list of users.
  • Query params: ?page=1&per_page=25&sort=created_at&search=Jo
  • Response: 200 OK with JSON: { "data": [ {...} ], "meta": { "page":1, "per_page":25, "total":123 } }

POST /user/delete/5
  • Uses POST to delete — semantic mismatch.

✓ Use DELETE /users/5
Details:
  • DELETE /users/{id} — deletes the resource identified by id.
  • Response: 204 No Content on successful deletion (or 200 with body if you return representation).
  • If deletion is soft (mark as deleted), consider PATCH /users/{id} with { "status":"deleted" } and return 200/204.
  • Idempotency: DELETE should be idempotent — deleting an already-deleted resource returns 204 or 404 depending on your policy.
GET /createUser?name=Jo
  • GET used for a state-changing operation (creates a user) — violates safe/read-only guarantee.

✓ Use POST /users
Details:

PUT /updateAllUsers
  • Verb "updateAllUsers" in path and ambiguous semantics: replace all users? bulk update? unclear.
  • PUT semantics apply to a single resource (replace). For collections, semantics are different and can be risky.
  • No guidance for partial vs full update, error handling for partial failures, or idempotency.
✓ Several options depending on intent
If you meant "replace the entire users collection" (rare):
  • PUT /users with an array body that fully replaces the collection. Response: 200 or 204. Rare and dangerous for multi-tenant systems.
If you meant "bulk update some users":

  • Request: { "operations": [ { "id":1, "op":"replace", "path":"/role", "value":"admin" }, { "id":2, "op":"replace", "path":"/status","value":"active" } ] } or a simpler bulk payload { "updates": [ { "id":1, "role":"admin" }, ... ] }

  • Use PATCH for partial updates to an individual resource: PATCH /users/{id} with { "op":"replace", ... } or a JSON Merge Patch.

  • Always validate and return 409 for uniqueness conflicts.
PUT /users/{id}
Full replacement — client provides entire resource representation
Request body: full user object (except id, createdAt) 
{
  "name": "string",
  "email": "string",
  "role": "string"
}
Responses: 
200 OK — returns replaced user
201 Created — if server allows PUT to create when missing (optional policy)
400 Bad Request
Notes:
PUT is idempotent: multiple identical calls produce the same result as one.
DELETE /users/{id}
Delete a user
Responses: 
204 No Content — success (preferred)
404 Not Found
Notes:
  • Consider soft delete (mark as deleted) and return 200 with object state if you need recoverability/audit.
PATCH /users
Bulk partial updates (use carefully)
Request body: 
{
  "filter": { /* criteria selecting users */ },
  "patch": { "role": "new-role" }
}
Responses: 
200 OK
Body: { "updated": 42 }
Notes:
  • Bulk operations should be explicit and authenticated; return operation ids for async jobs when long-running.

Errors & Status Codes

{
  "400": "Bad Request — validation errors",
  "401": "Unauthorized — auth missing/invalid",
  "403": "Forbidden — insufficient permissions",
  "404": "Not Found",
  "409": "Conflict — e.g. duplicate unique field",
  "422": "Unprocessable Entity — domain rule prevents action",
  "429": "Too Many Requests — rate limiting",
  "500": "Internal Server Error"
}
Include a consistent error envelope:
{
  "error": {
    "status": 400,
    "code": "validation_error",
    "message": "Email is required",
    "details": { "email": "must be a valid email" }
  }
}

Best practices & Examples

  • Authentication: Use Authorization: Bearer & strong scopes/roles.
  • Rate limiting: return 429 with Retry-After.
  • Versioning: use /v1 in path or Accept header.
  • Hypermedia: include links for next/prev pages and related resources.
Example: Create then get user (curl) 
curl -X POST https://api.example.com/v1/users \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"name":"Jo","email":"jo@example.com","password":"P@ssw0rd"}'

# returns 201 with Location: /v1/users/{id}
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer TOKEN" https://api.example.com/v1/users/{id}
Example: Safe vs unsafe misuse 
Bad: GET /createUser?name=Jo  -- creates side effects using GET (wrong)

Good: POST /users { name: "Jo" }  -- POST used for create (correct)
